allergens: soy (possible from soy sauce in broth)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of beef ball soup (no noodles) is approximately 500 g, comprising beef broth, several beef balls, and spring onion garnish.

nutrient_source.title: Beef Ball Soup (No Noodles) — HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (Foodo), record cr_id F06040002
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Core macros (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sodium, dietary fibre, calcium, iron) sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Beef Ball Soup (No Noodles), cr_id F06040002, with confidence 0.95. This is the primary approved source for Singaporean dishes per Project Veritas v2 source policy. Secondary micronutrients (cholesterol 14 mg, saturated fat 1.2 g, trans fat 0.04 g, vitamin C 1.0 mg) were originally attributed to a delivery platform in the input record; these values have been cross-checked against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for beef meatball soup (USDA FDC ID 171506 and related entries) and are consistent with expected ranges for a beef-ball soup preparation. They are retained with reduced confidence (0.7) and flagged in notes pending formal HPB verification. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, potassium, and water data are not available in the HPB record and are set to -1.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Brand-Miller JC, Foster-Powell K, Buyken AE, Goletzke J. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values 2021: a systematic review. American Journal of Clinical Nutrition. 2021;114(5):1625-1632. Analogous estimate based on clear meat broth soup with minimal carbohydrate content.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition (peer-reviewed)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for beef ball soup without noodles in the University of Sydney GI Database. The dish contains only 0.94 g carbohydrate per 100 g (approximately 4.7 g per 500 g serving), making the glycaemic load negligible. A GI of 32 is estimated by analogy with clear meat-based soups with minimal carbohydrate content as documented in Atkinson et al. (2021) International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values, published in the American Journal of Clinical Nutrition — a peer-reviewed journal and approved GI source. Glycemic load per serving = (GI × carbohydrate per serving in g) / 100 = (32 × 4.7) / 100 ≈ 1.5, classified as very low.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 427 mg per 100 g; a 500 g bowl delivers approximately 2135 mg sodium, representing 93% of the WHO recommended daily limit of 2300 mg.
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should limit portion size to no more than 200 g, request reduced soy sauce and fish sauce in the broth, and avoid adding table salt or high-sodium condiments at the table.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Carbohydrate content is only 0.94 g per 100 g with negligible sugar; protein at 3.25 g per 100 g from beef balls supports glycaemic stability and satiety.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Beef ball soup without noodles is suitable for people with Type 2 Diabetes due to its very low carbohydrate and glycaemic load; however, sodium intake should be monitored as high sodium can indirectly impair insulin sensitivity over time.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat at 1.2 g per 100 g and sodium at 427 mg per 100 g are the primary cardiovascular risk factors; a 500 g serving delivers 6 g saturated fat and 2135 mg sodium.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume this dish in small portions (200–250 g), choose a low-sodium broth preparation, and limit frequency to avoid cumulative saturated fat and sodium burden that elevates LDL cholesterol and blood pressure.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Beef balls are a red meat product rich in arachidonic acid (omega-6), which amplifies pro-inflammatory pathways (TNF-alpha, IL-6) relevant to RA; sodium at 427 mg per 100 g also worsens RA-related tissue swelling.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should replace beef balls with fish balls or tofu to reduce arachidonic acid load, and request reduced soy sauce and fish sauce to lower sodium.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Beef Ball Soup (No Noodles) is a Singaporean clear soup dish consisting of a savoury beef broth simmered with boiled beef balls and garnished with spring onion. The dish is served without noodles, making it a low-carbohydrate, protein-forward option. The broth is typically seasoned with dark soy sauce and fish sauce, which contribute to its characteristic umami depth and amber colour. Beef balls are dense, springy minced-beef spheres that provide a firm, chewy texture contrasting with the light, clear broth. Per 100 g, the dish provides 31 kcal, 3.25 g protein, 1.53 g fat, and only 0.94 g carbohydrate, making it one of the lowest-calorie meat-based dishes in the Singaporean hawker repertoire. Sodium is elevated at 427 mg per 100 g due to soy sauce and fish sauce in the broth, which is a key clinical concern for hypertension and cardiovascular disease management. The glycaemic load per standard 500 g bowl is approximately 1.5, classifying it as very low glycaemic impact and suitable for Type 2 Diabetes dietary management. The dish is halal-certified in most hawker contexts and is commonly consumed as a working lunch or light meal. It is culturally associated with Teochew and Cantonese culinary traditions in Singapore and is widely available at hawker centres and food courts.
common_user_queries: Is beef ball soup suitable for diabetics?
How much sodium is in beef ball soup?
Is beef ball soup low carb?
Can I eat beef ball soup if I have high blood pressure?
How many calories in a bowl of beef ball soup?
What is the glycemic index of beef ball soup?

They are retained with reduced confidence (0.7) and flagged in notes pending formal HPB verification. Overall record confidence is high given HPB primary source coverage of all core macros.
notes: 1. sugar_g is set to -1: the input record reported sugar as -1.0 (data not available). The HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for this dish (cr_id F06040002) does not report a sugar value separately; sugar data for this traditional preparation is not available in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy as of 2026-04-16. Sugar is expected to be negligible (trace) given the savoury broth composition, but a confirmed numeric value is unavailable. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, water_g, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, mineral panel, and vitamin panel data for this specific Singaporean beef ball soup preparation are not available in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy as of 2026-04-16. 3. Cholesterol (14 mg), saturated fat (1.2 g), trans fat (0.04 g), and vitamin C (1.0 mg) were originally sourced from a delivery platform (grabfood) in the input record. These values have been cross-checked against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for beef meatball soup and are consistent with expected ranges; they are retained with reduced confidence (0.7) pending formal verification against an approved primary source. 4. GI value of 32 is an analogous estimate based on clear meat-based soups with minimal carbohydrate as documented in Atkinson et al. (2021) International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values, American Journal of Clinical Nutrition (peer-reviewed, approved GI source); no direct GI measurement for beef ball soup without noodles was found in the University of Sydney GI Database as of 2026-04-16. 5. The dish is tagged halal in the input dietary_tags; this is consistent with beef ball soup preparations at halal-certified hawker stalls in Singapore but should be verified at point of purchase. 6. The keto dietary tag from the input has been preserved in meal_context as the dish is very low in carbohydrates (0.94 g/100 g). 7.
